coredata.dc:description: Combining the light-harvesting capabilities of photosensitizers with microbial catalysis shows great potential in solar-driven biomanufacturing. However, little information is available about the effects of photosensitizers on the photoelectron transport during the dissimilatory nitrate reduction to ammonium (DNRA) process. Herein, redox carbon dots (CDs-500) were prepared from sludge via the pyrolysis-Fenton reaction and then used to construct a photosynthetic system with Shewanella oneidensis MR-1. The MR-1/CDs-500 photosynthetic system showed a 5.9-fold increase in ammonia production (4.9 mmol(NH3)·g−1(protein)·h−1) with a high selectivity of 94.0 %. The photoelectrons were found to be stored in CDs-500 and transferred into the cells. During the inward electron transport, the intracellular CDs-500 could be used as the direct photoelectron transfer stations between outer membrane cytochrome c and DNRA-related enzymes without the involvement of CymA and MtrA. This work provides a new method for converting waste into functional catalysts and increases solar-driven NH3 production to a greater extent.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–c) Visible-light-driven dissimilatory nitrate reduction to ammonium (DNRA) process in the presence of CDs pyrolyzed at different temperatures. (d–f) DNRA process in the presence of CDs-500 and nano-biochar-500 under dark/light conditions. (50 mg/L CDs, 50 mg/L nano-biochar-500).

(a) Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) release at 12 h in various systems. (b–k) The confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) merge imaging of strain MR-1/CDs-500 hybrid along the z direction (numbered successively b to k, 150 nm step increment, Ex 405 nm/Em 420–500 nm, scale bars 2 μm). (l) The reduction of c-Cyts under dark/light irradiation. (m) Fluorescence (FL) intensity of CDs-500 with/without c-Cyts. (n) Visible-light-driven NH3 production in the mutant MR-1 photosynthetic system. (o) Visible-light-driven NH3 production by the cells pre-incubated with CDs-500 (CDs-500-fed cells) and CDs-500-fed cells with extracellularly added CDs-500. (p) NH3 production of strain MR-1/CDs-500 under alternating light/dark conditions. (q) The resting cells tests under dark conditions. (ns p > 0.05, * p < 0.05, ** p ≤ 0.01, *** p ≤ 0.001).

(a) Lactate consumption of strain MR-1 in various systems. (b) Carbon flux distribution in different photosynthetic systems. (c) Nyquist plots of strain MR-1 in the presence of CDs-500 and nano-biochar-500. (d–g) Effects of CDs-500 on the Nap activities, NrfA activities, electron transport system activity (ETSA) and ATP levels of strain MR-1 after 12 h of incubation under the dark/light irradiation. (h) Flavin generation in strain MR-1/CDs-500 hybrid system. (i) Effect of flavins on NH3 production under light/dark conditions. (ns p > 0.05, ** p ≤ 0.01, *** p ≤ 0.001).

(a, b) Effects of CDs-500 on the NADH level and NDH activities of strain MR-1 after 12 h of incubation under the dark/light irradiation. (c) Protein contents in various systems. (d) The effects of CDs-500 on the gene expression of strain MR-1. Red, blue and gray represent upregulation, downregulation and non-significance, respectively. (e) Proposed electron transfer pathways for the light-driven NH3 production in the MR-1/CDs-500 photosynthetic system. (ns p > 0.05, *** p ≤ 0.001).

head.ce:title: Bifunctional sludge-derived redox carbon dots with photoelectron storage and delivery properties for ammonia production by photosensitized Shewanella oneidensis MR-1

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Combining the light-harvesting capabilities of photosensitizers with microbial catalysis shows great potential in solar-driven biomanufacturing. However, little information is available about the effects of photosensitizers on the photoelectron transport during the dissimilatory nitrate reduction to ammonium (DNRA) process. Herein, redox carbon dots (CDs-500) were prepared from sludge via the pyrolysis-Fenton reaction and then used to construct a photosynthetic system with Shewanella oneidensis MR-1. The MR-1/CDs-500 photosynthetic system showed a 5.9-fold increase in ammonia production (4.9 mmol(NH3)·g−1(protein)·h−1) with a high selectivity of 94.0 %. The photoelectrons were found to be stored in CDs-500 and transferred into the cells. During the inward electron transport, the intracellular CDs-500 could be used as the direct photoelectron transfer stations between outer membrane cytochrome c and DNRA-related enzymes without the involvement of CymA and MtrA. This work provides a new method for converting waste into functional catalysts and increases solar-driven NH3 production to a greater extent.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ammonia (NH3), an indispensable raw material for industrial production, relies heavily on the Haber-Bosch process. Harsh operating conditions (300–500 °C, 20–25 MPa) are involved in this process for the activation of stable N≡N bonds in nitrogen gas (N2) (Shi et al., 2023; Soloveichik, 2019). Unlike N2, nitrate (NO3 –) is of higher water solubility and has a lower N=O dissociation energy. Thus, NO3 – can be used as an alternative nitrogen source for NH3 production (Liang et al., 2023). NO3 – is also considered an environmental pollutant and poses a serious threat to human health (Wang et al., 2024). It has been found that efficient NH3 synthesis and NO3 – removal can be achieved simultaneously at room temperature using photocatalysis and biotechnology, which is important for alleviating the energy crisis and achieving carbon neutrality (Shi et al., 2023; Su et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2024). Photocatalytic NH3 production possesses the advantages of sustainability and low energy consumption, but photocatalysts suffer from high costs and poor substrate specificity (Xiao et al., 2021). Microbial dissimilatory nitrate reduction to ammonium (DNRA) is restricted by microbial metabolism rates, resulting in a slow production rate of NH3 (Su et al., 2020).

Photosynthetic biological hybrid systems, as emerging technologies, combine the excellent light-capturing ability of photocatalysts with the catalytic ability of non-photosynthetic bacteria, realizing the high conversion of solar energy to chemical energy (Shen et al., 2023; Cestellos-Blanco et al., 2020). Therefore, the development of biohybrids may be a very promising strategy for NH3 production. Among various photosensitizers, carbon dots (CDs) are known for their good optical properties, reliable biocompatibility, and low cost (Li et al., 2022a; Li et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2020). It has been found that CDs can accelerate the extracellular electron transfer (EET) process of Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 for light-mediated electricity generation or extracellular Cr (VI) reduction (Guo et al., 2019; Li et al., 2022b). The internalized CDs could not only enhance the conductivity and metabolism of Shewanella sp., but also accelerate the non-transmembrane intracellular photoelectron transfer processes of Escherichia coli for photocatalytic hydrogen production (Li et al., 2023a; Liu et al., 2020; Lv et al., 2024). However, extracellular CDs are usually easier to be photosensitized. Considering that the biosynthesis of NH3 by strain MR-1 is an intracellular multienzyme-catalyzed process unlike EET, the potential of CDs as photosensitizers for the biosynthesis of NH3 and CDs-mediated transmembrane photoelectron transport process needs to be further investigated. CymA and MtrA usually play indispensable roles in the typical reversing metal-reducing (Mtr) system of strain MR-1 for NH3 production (Li et al., 2023b; Rowe et al., 2021; Schwalb et al., 2003). The long-distance electron transfer involved in multi-enzymes may decrease the electron transfer rate. Based on the internalization of CDs and redox properties of these enzymes (Han et al., 2022; Li et al., 2024), redox CDs were assumed to be potential hubs for increasing NH3 production to a greater extent.

In recent decades, rapid urbanization and industrialization have resulted in the accumulation of large quantities of sludge waste. It was reported that this excess sludge could be converted into functional carbon-based materials through pyrolysis under restricted oxygen conditions (Chen et al., 2020; Duan et al., 2023). As some excess sludges contain a certain concentration of iron, and Fe(III) can be reduced to Fe(II)/Fe(0) during sludge pyrolysis (Chen et al., 2020), these Fe-doped biochars can be used to drive Fenton reaction for environmental remediation. However, little attention has been paid to the self-catalytic cleavage of such bulk biochar to produce nano-biochar with redox groups during the Fenton process. In the present study, Fe doped biochars were synthesized from Fe-containing sludge via pyrolysis at 300 ℃, 500 ℃ and 700 ℃, and were used as precursors to drive the Fenton reaction for the synthesis of CDs-300, CDs-500 and CDs-700, respectively. Shewanella oneidensis MR-1, a typical bacterium of DNRA, was used as the model bacterium for NH3 production. This work demonstrated that CDs-500 with certain redox properties and the highest electronic conductivity exhibited the greatest enhancement effect on electron transfer for NO3 – removal and NH3 production. Extracellular CDs-500 were found to store and transfer photoelectrons into cells. The partial photoelectrons were used for biomass synthesis through the typical reversing Mtr system. More importantly, intracellular CDs-500 provided a direct photoelectron transfer pathway from outer membrane cytochrome c (OM c-Cyts) to DNRA-related enzymes without MtrA and CymA involvement, indicating that a short-range atypical photoelectron transfer pathway was provided for rapid photoelectron transport and ammonia production. The present work provided a pyrolysis-Fenton reaction method for the conversion of sludge to CDs with redox properties, and constructed a low-cost and metal-free photosynthetic system to produce NH3.

All reagents used in this work were of analytical grade and used without additional purification. Sludge was obtained from the secondary sedimentation tank of the Lingshuihe wastewater treatment plant (Dalian, China). The ΔmtrC and ΔmtrA mutants were kindly provided by Prof. Haichun Gao from Zhejiang University (Zhang et al., 2020). The ΔmtrCΔomcA mutant strain was kindly provided by Prof. Guoping Sheng and Prof. Hanqing Yu from the University of Science and Technology of China (Han et al., 2022).

The initial sludge contained Fe (4.4 %), Ni (0.02 %), Cu (0.05 %), Mn (0.03 %), Zn (0.09 %), Cr (0.01 %), Ti (0.39 %) and Al (2.05 %). To remove excess metals, the initial sludge was first hydrolyzed at 80 °C and pH 3 for 3.5 h. Then acid-hydrolyzed sludge was dried at 105 °C and ground into a fine powder. Finally, under an N2 atmosphere and a heating rate of 5 ℃/min, the powder was heated at 300, 500, and 700 ℃ for 3 h to obtain bulk biochar-300, biochar-500 and biochar-700, respectively.

CDs were synthesized by an in-situ Fenton reaction (top-down). Typically, 0.05 g of bulk sludge-derived biochar was first washed two times with 3 M HCl and water (acid-washed biochar), and then mixed with 5 mL of 6 % H2O2. The mixture was incubated at 30 ℃ and 150 r/min for 24 h, centrifuged (10 000 g, 10 min) and then filtered through 0.1 μm filter membranes to remove the bulk biochar. The obtained black supernatant was first purified in a 1 KD dialysis bag for 12 h to remove ions and H2O2, and then transferred into a 100 KD dialysis bag, where the outside was CDs (<10 nm) and the inside was nano-biochar (10–100 nm). The obtained solutions were further freeze-dried for subsequent experiments. The CDs obtained from bulk biochar-300, biochar-500 and biochar-700 were named CDs-300, CDs-500 and CDs-700, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and assembly of MR-1/CDs hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: Strain MR-1 was first grown (30 ℃, 150 r/min) in Luria-Bertani (LB) medium (10 g/L NaCl, 10 g/L tryptone and 5 g/L yeast extract) until it reached the logarithmic stationary phase. The cells were washed three times with 10 mM PBS (1.44 g/L Na2HPO4, 0.24 g/L KH2PO4, 8 g/L NaCl and 0.2 g/L KCl), collected by centrifugation (10 000 g, 5 min) and resuspended in the 10 mL of PBS. Twenty milliliters of anaerobic medium (AM, 2.24 g/L C3H5O3Na, 0.425 g/L NaNO3, 0.02 g/L CaCl2, 7.22 g/L K2HPO4, 1.16 g/L KH2PO4 and 0.10 g MgSO4, pH 7.5) were added to transparent serum bottles (each, 25 mL) and purged with N2 for 15 min. These bottles were then sealed with butyl stoppers and autoclaved at 121 ℃ for 20 min. Then, the cells and CDs were separately added into the serum bottles with syringes in an anaerobic incubator. The hybrids of MR-1 and the CDs were prepared by simply mixing the cells (with an OD600 of 0.1) and the CDs (25–250 mg/L).

ce:section.ce:section-title: NH3 production of biohybrids under visible light
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic tests were conducted under cold-white light emitting diode (LED) irradiation (3.7 mW cm−2, 400–750 nm). The light intensity and spectrogram were measured by a spectral analyzer (PLA-30, EVERFINE, China). Lactate in AM was both an electron donor for cell metabolism and a hole scavenger for the CDs. The above hybrids were incubated at 30 ℃ at 150 rpm under LED irradiation and samples were collected at certain time intervals to monitor the variation in the concentrations of NO3 –, NO2 – and NH4 +.

Photoelectric tests were carried out under a three-electrode system. Pt plate and saturated calomel electrode were used as the counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The samples mixed with Nafion solution (1.7 %) were coated on the working electrode. The working electrode for the measurements of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS)/cyclic voltammetry (CV) and transient photocurrent response was a glassy carbon electrode and ITO glass, respectively. KCl (0.1 M) aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte. AC-EIS data were obtained in the frequency range of 1–100 kHz (5 mV amplitude). Transient photocurrent response tests were performed under cold-white LED irradiation (56.4 mW cm−2). CV curves were measured at a scan rate of 10 mV.

Under visible light irradiation (LED, 29.6 mW cm−2) or in the dark. 4 mL of PBS solution (50 mM, pH 7.5) containing 100 mg/L c-Cyts, 100 mg/L CDs-500 and 20 mM lactate was incubated at 30 ℃ for 1 h. The photoreduction of c-Cyts was measured by monitoring the increased absorbance at 550 nm, where the molar absorption coefficient of reduced c-Cyts was calculated by the complete reduction with Na2S2O4.

Cells (an OD600 nm of 0.1) with or without CDs-500 (50 mg/L) were pre-cultured under dark conditions and then collected. CDs-500 (1 mg) were firstly added to 20 mL of anaerobic medium and exposed to visible light (3.7 mW cm−2) for 2 h. Finally, the collected cells (a final OD600 nm of 0.5) and nitrite (a final concentration of 2.5 mM) were added to the above medium and incubated for 1 h at 30 ℃. 150 r/min.

The concentrations of NO3 –, NO2 – and NH4 + were measured by the spectrophotometry methods (Li et al., 2024). Protein concentration was determined by the Bradford method. Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) release, ATP and NADH levels were measured by a cytotoxicity detection kit (Leagene, China), microbial cell activity assay kit (BacTiter Glo, Germany) and NAD+/NADH assay kit (Beyotime, China), respectively. The concentration of extracellular CDs in the supernatant was measured by detecting the absorbance at 600 nm. The electron transport system activity (ETSA), cell disruption and enzyme activity assays referred to a previous study (Li et al., 2024).

The concentration of lactate was measured by using the high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) with an SPD-M20A UV detector (Shimadzu, Japan) and a Welch Ultimate®polar-RP (4.6 × 250 mm, 5 µm) column. The chromatographic conditions were as follows: 10 μL of injection volume, a flow rate of 0.5 mL/min, 30 ℃, 0.1 % phosphoric acid in water, and detection wavelength of 210 nm.

The concentration of flavins was determined by using the HPLC with an RF-20A fluorescence detector (Shimadzu, Japan) and a Sepax C18 column (4.6 × 250 mm, 5 μm). The chromatographic conditions were as follows: 10 μL of injection volume, a flow rate of 1 mL/min, 40 ℃, 35 % methanol/ 65 % water containing 5 mM ammonium acetate and 0.2 % acetic acid, excitation wavelength of 424 nm and emission wavelength of 526 nm.

The sludge was carbonized in a tube furnace (BTF-1700C, BEST, China) filled with N2. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, USA) was used to investigate the morphology and size of the CDs. The elemental composition, structure and functional groups of the CDs were studied by using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Escalab 250Xi, USA), X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Dmax Ultima+, Japan) and Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR, Thermo IS50, USA). The electrochemical properties and optical absorption of the samples were measured using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660D, Chenhua, China) and a UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectrometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. The absorbance results were obtained by using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-6100, Mapada, China). The fluorescence (FL) intensity of the CDs was measured by a fluorescence spectrometer (Agilent Cary Eclipse, USA). The concentrations of metal elements were determined by an inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES, Avio 220, PerkinElmer, USA). A confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, Zeiss, LSM980, Germany) was used for the fluorescence imaging in vivo. The conductivity was measured by a conductivity meter (METTLER TOLEDO, SG3, Switzerland). The fixed and dehydrated bio-samples were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, EOL, JSM-7610 Plus, Japan).

After 12 h of incubation under visible light, both the strain MR-1 and the MR-1/CDs-500 hybrid were collected in triplicate for transcriptome studies. Detailed information includes RNA extraction, quality control, library preparation, sequencing, and bioinformatics analysis (Zhang et al., 2022). The raw transcriptomic reads were deposited to the NCBI Sequence Read Archive under accession number PRJNA1085415.

All experiments were repeated in triplicate, and the data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation. GraphPad Prism 8.0.2 software was used to analyze the significance of the results by the two-tailed Student’s t test, and p < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of sludge-derived CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The initial sludge was first acid-hydrolyzed. There were 2.5 % Fe, 0.3 % Ti and 0.9 % Al in the acid-hydrolyzed sludge. Then it was pyrolyzed at 300 ℃, 500 ℃ and 700 ℃ for the preparation of three bulk biochars. XRD patterns showed that Fe in the bulk biochars was amorphous (see Supplementary Material). The formed Fe(II) during pyrolysis were used as reactive sites to drive the subsequent Fenton reaction (see Supplementary Material). Then acid washing was conducted to further remove these excess metals (see Supplementary Material) and provided an acid environment. After H2O2 addition, the generated ·OH cleaved the three bulk biochars into CDs-300, CDs-500, and CDs-700 (see Supplementary Material). TEM images (see Supplementary Material) showed that the average particle sizes of CDs-300, CDs-500, and CDs-700 were 2.1, 1.7 and 2.6 nm, respectively. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images and XRD pattern revealed that CDs-500 had a clearer graphite lattice (100) and sharper graphite diffraction peak (002) compared to CDs-300 and CDs-700 (see Supplementary Material). Furthermore, the typical hexagonal honeycomb structure of graphite was observed only in the HRTEM image of CDs-500 (see Supplementary Material), indicating that CDs-500 were of better graphitization than CDs-300 and CDs-700.

Since CDs could function as electronic mediators, the electronic conductivities of the three CDs were compared. The results showed the following order: CDs-300 (0.9 mS/cm) < CDs-700 (1.3 mS/cm) < CDs-500 (2.2 mS/cm) (see Supplementary Material), which was mainly dependent on Fe(II) content in their precursor bulk biochars. Generally, the higher pyrolysis temperature induced a greater degree of iron reduction, which facilitated the subsequent activation of H2O2 for more ·OH generation to cleave the bulk biochar. In the present study, the Fe(II) content of biochar-700 (1.2 %) was higher compared with those of biochar-500 (0.4 %) and biochar-300 (0.1 %) based on XPS results (see Supplementary Material), resulting in the intensity of the Fenton reaction followed the order: biochar-300 < biochar-500 < biochar-700 (see Supplementary Material). Since the intense Fenton reaction could also decompose the graphite structure of generated CDs, it was observed that the yield of CDs-700 was lower than that of CDs-500 (see Supplementary Material). Furthermore, the O content of CDs-700 was the highest with 50.1 %, followed by CDs-500 (34.2 %) and CDs-300 (32.4 %) (see Supplementary Material). The excessive oxygenated groups in CDs-700 could break their aromaticity and result in a lower electrical conductivity compared with that of CDs-500.

XPS survey analysis (see Supplementary Material) revealed that the CDs mainly consisted of C, N and O elements. As the pyrolysis temperature increased from 300 ℃ to 700 ℃, the N content of CDs gradually decreased from 8.3 % to 5.3 %. The N element in the XPS N 1 s spectra (see Supplementary Material) could be fitted with three states, i.e., pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N (Liu et al., 2023). For CDs-300, the peaks at 398.5 eV, 399.8 eV and 400.7 eV could be attributed to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively, which accounted for 2.2 %, 5.4 % and 0.7 % of total elements, respectively. Since more electronegative O could decrease the electron density of N, the three N species all shifted to higher binding energies in CDs-500/CDs-700, and the contents of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N were calculated to be 3.7 %/3.1 %, 1.3 %/0.4 % and 2.1 %/1.8 %, respectively. Previous studies have shown that graphitization and N doping can enhance the light absorption and charge transfer capacity of CDs (Martindale et al., 2017). Especially, catalysts with a higher content of pyridinic-N or graphitic-N exhibited a higher electrocatalytic activity (Domínguez et al., 2015; Niwa et al., 2009). It was assumed that CDs-500 had better electrocatalytic activity than CDs-700 and CDs-300.

Meanwhile, it was observed that, compared to the FT-IR spectrum of CDs-300, the new peaks at 3070 cm−1 and 1260 cm−1 were observed in the spectra of CDs-500 and CDs-700 (see Supplementary Material), which corresponded to aromatic C–H and aromatic C–O stretching vibrations, respectively. Furthermore, higher intensity of aromatic ketones (C=O) peaks at 1690 cm−1 was observed in CDs-500/CDs-700. Combined with the analysis of XPS C 1 s and XPS O 1 s spectra (see Supplementary Material), it was found that the proportions of C=O in the three CDs followed the order: CDs-300 < CDs-500 < CDs-700. Further analysis showed that the redox peaks of CDs-700 (80 mV and −300 mV) were slightly more obvious and reversible than that of CDs-500 in CV curves (see Supplementary Material). The above results indicated that these oxygen-containing groups (C=O) on aromatic structures endowed CDs-700 with better redox properties compared with CDs-500.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visible-light-assisted NH3 production of the strain MR-1/CDs hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of the CDs prepared at three temperatures on photocatalytic NH3 production were further compared (Fig. 1 a–c). The results showed that, under visible light irradiation, the accelerating effect of CDs-500 on nitrate removal and NH3 production was the greatest, followed by those of CDs-700 and CDs-300. According to the structural characterization of the three CDs, it could be concluded that CDs-500 with certain redox properties and the highest electronic conductivity contributed to the enhancement of photocatalytic activity. The Zeta potentials analysis showed that the three CDs followed the order: |ζCDs-500| (−9.9 ± 0.6 mV) < |ζCDs-700| (−26.3 ± 0.2 mV) < |ζCDs-300| (–33.0 ± 0.2 mV), indicating that CDs with less surface charges exhibited a better photocatalytic performance. As the zeta potential of strain MR-1 was negative (−4.3 ± 0.5 mV), the more negative surface charge of the CDs enhanced the electrostatic repulsion between the CDs and strain MR-1, which was not conducive to interactions between CDs and cells’ OM. Therefore, CDs-500 were chosen for NH3 production. Further analysis showed that CDs-500 could be excited under different excitation wavelengths and were found to be of good optical stability at different pH values, temperatures, and ionic strengths (see Supplementary Material), indicating that CDs-500 were good photosensitizers.

Further studies revealed that CDs-500 alone and CDs-500 with dead cells failed to reduce nitrate, indicating that NH3 production must involve active biological enzymes (Fig. 1d–f). Among the tested concentration range of additional CDs-500, 50 mg/L CDs-500 exhibited the best accelerating performance (see Supplementary Material). During this process, the nitrate removal efficiency significantly increased by 30.1 % at 5 h under visible light irradiation compared to that of the bare cells (control group) (Fig. 1d–f). Moreover, with the decrease of nitrite concentration, NH3 production gradually increased and reached a maximum (4.7 mM) at 12 h with 94.0 % selectivity, which was 5.9-fold higher than that of the control (0.8 mM, 16.0 %). In comparison, there was no significant accelerating effect of CDs-500 on NH3 production under dark conditions (see Supplementary Material), indicating that the NH3 production of the bio-hybrids could be driven by visible light. LDH release assays were carried out to evaluate the cytotoxicity of CDs-500 to strain MR-1 under dark or visible light conditions. Fig. 2 a showed that CDs-500 addition did not result in an increase in LDH release, demonstrating the good biocompatibility of CDs-500. Moreover, transition metals such as Fe, Ni, Cu, Mn, Co, Cr, Al and Ti were not detected in CDs-500 based on the results of XPS and ICP-OES.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the strain MR-1/CDs-500 hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: For strain MR-1/50 mg/L CDs-500-amended system, the cells were brown in color compared to the pink color of the bare cells (see Supplementary Material). Furthermore, SEM images showed that a large number of nanoscale particles were located on the surface of strain MR-1 (see Supplementary Material). Using CLSM, the distribution of CDs-500 in cells was observed (Fig. 2b–k and Supplementary Material). With a large step increment of 150 nm, when the laser passed through the surface-center-surface of the cells, an obvious FL signal of CDs-500 could be detected on the surface or inside the cells, indicating that the CDs-500 could attach to the OM or enter the cells.

Further analysis revealed that CDs-500 exhibited strong absorption in the visible region with an absorption edge of approximately 500 nm using an ultraviolet−visible diffuse reflection spectrometer (see Supplementary Material). Based on the Tauc plot (Han et al., 2022), the band gap of CDs-500 was calculated to be 2.50 eV (see Supplementary Material). XPS valence band spectrum showed that the valence band (VB) of CDs-500 was 2.05 V (see Supplementary Material), and the conduction band (CB) was calculated to be −0.45 V (ECB=EVB-Eg). According to the EIS Nyquist plots (see Supplementary Material), strain MR-1/CDs-500 hybrid exhibited a smaller radius than bare cells, which confirmed the rapid interfacial charge transfer of strain MR-1/CDs-500 hybrid. The embedded equivalent circuit fitted by Zview software exhibited that the charge transfer resistance (RCT) of cells and strain MR-1/CDs-500 hybrid were 4756 Ω and 3395 Ω, respectively. Under visible light irradiation, strain MR-1/CDs-500 hybrid exhibited a higher photocurrent intensity than bare cells alone and CDs-500 (see Supplementary Material). Furthermore, the FL intensity of strain MR-1/CDs-500 hybrid decreased compared to that of CDs-500 (see Supplementary Material), further confirming the presence of photogenerated electron transfer between the cells and CDs-500.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electron generation, flow and distribution in strain MR-1/CDs-500 photosynthetic system
ce:section.ce:para: The OM c-Cyts (e.g., OmcA, MtrA, MtrB and MtrC) are reportedly electron acceptors that can promote the separation of photoelectron-hole pairs in photocatalysts (Yi et al., 2022). Thermodynamically, the CB of CDs-500 (−450 mV) was more negative than the reduction potentials of MtrC (−0.5 to −277.5 mV vs SHE) and OmcA (−25 to −325 mV vs SHE), (Firer-Sherwood et al., 2008; Han et al., 2022) indicating that the photoinduced electrons could be transferred from CDs-500 to OM c-Cyts. Fig. 2l, m demonstrated that c-Cyts could be chelated with CDs-500 and reduced under visible light irradiation, which provided the possibility of reversing electron transport from OM c-Cyts to DNRA-related enzymes. To verify this possibility, mutants lacking OM c-Cyts (e.g., ΔmtrC and ΔmtrCΔomcA) were used (Fig. 2n). Compared to the wild-type strain MR-1-amended group, the knockout of gene mtrC alone had no apparent effect on the DNRA efficiency without CDs-500. However, the NH3 production in ΔmtrC mutant/CDs-500 hybrid system was 3.1 mM at 12 h, which was lower than that in strain MR-1/CDs-500 hybrid system (4.7 mM). For ΔomcAΔmtrC mutant-amended group, the NH3 production in the presence of CDs-500 was only 0.9 mM. These results indicated that OmcA and MtrC play important roles in the hybrid photosynthetic system. When 50 mg/L CDs-500 were co-cultured with strain MR-1 for 12 h, the concentration of extracellular CDs-500 was only 35 mg/L. After removing the extracellular CDs-500, it was observed that there was no significant increase in NH3 production by the CDs-500-fed cells (Fig. 2o), which, however, significantly increased to 4.8 mM after extracellular addition of 35 mg/L CDs-500. These results implied that intracellular CDs-500 mainly responded to the photoelectrons from extracellular CDs-500 via OM c-Cyts.

Thermodynamically, extracellular redox CDs-500 can be also self-photoreduced for photoelectron storage. Even under alternating light/dark conditions (Fig. 2p), the NH3 production efficiency (3.4 mM) by strain MR-1/CDs-500 hybrid achieved over half of that under continuous light conditions (4.7 mM). The resting cells tests (Fig. 2q) have demonstrated that the pre-photoreduced CDs-500 can release the stored photoelectrons for enhanced NH3 production (especially the cells absorbing CDs-500) under dark conditions. These results indicated that the redox CDs-500 could be used as mediators for photoelectron transfer during the DNRA process.

Further analysis showed that strain MR-1 alone and strain MR-1/CDs-500 hybrid systems consumed 3.7 mM and 6.1 mM lactate to produce 0.8 and 4.7 mM NH3 in 12 h (Fig. 3 a), respectively, indicating that CDs-500 addition resulted in the consumption of less lactate for the equal NH3 production. Based on the lactate consumption and NH3 production, the detailed carbon flux distribution could be calculated. Fig. 3b shows that, under visible light irradiation, theoretically, only 32.2 % of the lactate was used for DNRA process in the control group, while this percentage significantly increased to 52.5 % after the addition of CDs-500. It was also found that the lack of OM c-Cyts reduced the carbon flux distribution to DNRA. In particular, in the ΔmtrCΔomcA/CDs-500 hybrid, the lactate concentration used for DNRA decreased to the control level, indicating that OM c-Cyts and lactate were also used as photoelectron acceptors and hole (h+) scavengers, respectively, for the rapid carrier separation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Roles of intracellular CDs-500 in visible-light-driven NH3 production
ce:section.ce:para: Further analysis found that, in the presence of extracellular nano-biochar-500 with a larger particle size (20 nm) (see Supplementary Material), the NH3 production was much lower than that in the presence of CDs-500 (Fig. 1d–f), suggesting that it did not work well when only extracellular nanocarbon was available. This might be because CDs-500 with a smaller particle size (1.7 nm) could enter cells, which could more significantly reduce the Rct of bacteria than that of extracellular nano-biochar-500 (Fig. 3c). Furthermore, due to the low oxidation potential of CDs-500 (80 mV) in CV curves (see Supplementary Material), intracellular CDs-500 could be reduced and accepted the photoelectrons from OM c-Cyts and influenced the electron transport pathway.

CymA and MtrA usually play integral roles in the typical bidirectional metal-reducing (Mtr) system of strain MR-1 for DNRA (Li et al., 2023b; Rowe et al., 2021; Schwalb et al., 2003). Since CDs-500 failed to accelerate NH3 production under dark conditions (see Supplementary Material), this suggested that CDs-500 were unable to transport electrons from CymA to DNRA-related enzymes. To confirm the direction of intracellular photoelectron transfer, the ΔmtrA mutant was used because the MtrA protein is responsible for the electron transport from the OM c-Cyts to the CymA in the typical reversing electron transfer chain. In the ΔmtrA mutant-mediated photosynthetic system (Fig. 2n), the addition of CDs-500 resulted in 1.5 mM increase in NH3 production, suggesting that intracellular CDs-500 could act as electron shuttles to directly transfer photoelectrons from OM c-Cyts to DNRA-related enzymes without the involvement of MtrA and CymA. Further analysis showed that periplasmic nitrate reductase (Nap) activity, and ammonia-forming cytochrome c nitrite reductase (NrfA) activity were significantly increased by 1.5-fold and 1.6-fold, respectively, in the presence of CDs-500 (Fig. 3d, e). Considering the reduction potential of the reduced CDs-500 (−300 mV) (see Supplementary Material) and the interaction between the c-Cyts and CDs-500 (Fig. 2l, m), it was assumed that NapB (−150 to −350 mV vs SHE) (Jepson et al., 2007) and NrfA (>−120 mV vs SHE) (Ali et al., 2019) with c-Cyts structures might combine with CDs-500 and then directly reduced by the reduced CDs-500 to produce NH3. The above results indicated that intracellular CDs-500 can provide a short-range atypical photoelectron transfer pathway for NH3 production, resulting in enhanced ETSA and ATP levels in the cells (Fig. 3f, g). Moreover, the addition of CDs-500 stimulated the synthesis and secretion of flavins. The extracellular concentration of flavins increased from 2.0 to 18.1 nM (Fig. 3h). Since flavins (−219 mV vs SHE) can be thermodynamically reduced by CDs-500 and act as soluble redox mediators to mediate electron transfer, the role of flavins was also investigated. Fig. 3i show that the addition of 18 mM FMN increased NH3 production by approximately 0.7 mM compared to that in the control group. Perhaps the CDs-500/flavins-mediated electron transfer pathways appeared in strain MR-1/CDs-500 photosynthetic system. The level of intracellular NADH significantly decreased and there was no significant enhancement in NDH activity compared with that in the control without CDs-500 (Fig. 4 a, b). However, it was observed that the addition of CDs-500 increased the biomass production (Fig. 4c). Moreover, mtrA gene deletion resulted in a significant decrease in biomass in CDs-500-mediated photosynthetic system, indicating that MtrA could transfer photoelectrons to supply reducing power for biomass synthesis. The increased biomass also contributed to increased NH3 production. According to the calculation (see Supplementary Material), the photoelectrons flowing to DNRA-related enzymes and biomass synthesis were responsible for 46.9 % and 24.5 % of the total NH3 production, respectively. Since there was no significant biomass change in the bare cell-amended system, the bio-metabolism electrons are responsible for 28.6 % of the total NH3 production.

To further reveal the effect of CDs-500 on the biochemical metabolic pathways of strain MR-1 under visible light, the transcriptome was used to analyze the expression of relevant genes. Illumina RNA-Seq data showed that, under visible light irradiation, the additional CDs-500 resulted in the upregulation of 731 genes (p < 0.05, log2 FC≥1) and the downregulation of 653 genes (p < 0.05, log2 FC≤−1), respectively. Based on the Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) annotation, the differentially expressed genes were mainly related to carbon metabolism, nitrate metabolism, sulfate metabolism, amino acid synthesis, lipid metabolism, flavin synthesis, type IV pilus/flagellar assembly and the Mtr system (Fig. 4d and Supplementary Material).

Bacterial nanowires and the Mtr system are thought to provide EET pathways for transporting metabolic electrons to external electron acceptors (Han et al., 2022; Pirbadian et al., 2014). The transcriptome data showed that the genes related to the Mtr system (i.e., MtrA, MtrB, MtrC, OmcA and cctA), type IV pilus assembly and flavin synthesis were significantly upregulated in strain MR-1/CDs-500 photosynthetic system, which facilitated the enhancement of ETSA (Figs. 3f and 4d). Furthermore, the genes encoding NapB and NrfA were significantly upregulated 3.5- and 9.2-fold, respectively in CDs-500-amended group, which was conducive to removing nitrate/nitrite for increased NH3 production.

The above results indicated that the CDs-500 under visible irradiation could regulate the carbon flux distribution of strain MR-1. Based on transcriptome data (Fig. 4d and Supplementary Material), the genes encoding lactate dehydrogenase (Ldh), glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GapA), isocitrate dehydrogenase (Icd) and malate dehydrogenase (Mdh) for NADH generation and the gene encoding NDH for NADH conversion were not significantly expressed, further demonstrating that metabolic electrons were not the only reducing power for NH3 production. This result could be attributed to the short-distance photoelectron transfer process. To investigate the flow of metabolic electrons, the overall carbon metabolism was analyzed. Most genes related to the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle, Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas (EMP) and phosphate pathway (PPP), were significantly upregulated after the addition of CDs-500 under visible light irradiation (see Supplementary Material); these genes are crucial for controlling cell metabolism. The genes encoding enzymes involved in sulfate/nitrogen assimilation, amino acid synthesis, fatty acid synthesis and phospholipid synthesis were also significantly upregulated (see Supplementary Material). These processes consumed large amounts of electrons. As a result of the imbalance between the decreased NADH content (Fig. 4a) and the increased biomass synthesis (Fig. 4c) in the MR-1/CDs-500 photosynthetic system, the photoelectrons were supplied to the reducing force. In addition, the genes related to ATP synthase and the Pta-Ak pathway in the acetate synthesis step were found to be significantly upregulated in the MR-1/CDs-500 photosynthetic system. The subsequent increase in ATP level favored bacterial chemotaxis (Figs. 3g and 4d), and the gene encoding flagellar assembly was also significantly upregulated. Bacterial chemotaxis increased the possibility of bacterial contact with nitrate, nitrite, CDs-500 and light (Pandey and Jain, 2002; Zhang et al., 2022), which facilitated more photoelectron transport for NH3 production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed electron transfer pathways of visible-light-driven NH3 production
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above results, the corresponding electron transfer pathways for NH3 production by the MR-1/CDs-500 photosynthetic system were proposed (Fig. 4e). There were three main electron transport pathways. Firstly, extracellular CDs-500 were excited by visible light, and then the produced photoelectrons mainly flowed to two directions. The photoelectrons could be directly transferred from OM c-Cyts to the intracellular DNRA-related enzymes without the involvement of CymA and MtrA (path 1, 46.9 %), during which CDs-500 and flavins were used as electron mediators. Alternatively, the photoelectrons can be transferred from MtrA to CymA for biomass synthesis (path 2, 24.5 %). Lactate could be biologically metabolized to NADH, which then provided a reducing power for DNRA under the catalysis of NDH (path 3, 28.6 %).

It was reported that NH3 production efficiency was less than 5 % using Escherichia coli (Su et al., 2022) and Desulfovibrio sp. (Su et al., 2020) in 12 h under anaerobic conditions. In comparison, Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 exhibited better DNRA performance with 16 % NH3 production efficiency in 12 h. Even so, its NH3 production rate was still low. A previous study has reported that the microbial electrochemical system can be applied for NH3 production via the typical reversing Mtr system of strain MR-1 (Li et al., 2023b). Its NH3 production efficiency reached 72 % (82.5 % selectivity) in 120 h. As the photoelectrons can be also transported by the typical reversing Mtr system (Han et al., 2022), the photo-hybrid systems have great potential in NH3 production. Currently, the main photosensitizers for the construction of biohybrids are usually some inorganic semiconductor materials such as CdS/CdSe, gold nanoclusters, and InP/ZnSe quantum dots (Cestellos-Blanco et al., 2020; Shen et al., 2023), which are mainly related to hydrogen production, denitrification, CO2 reduction and N2 fixation. However, their high preparation cost and the biotoxicity of heavy metals released from these materials may limit their application in photosynthetic biohybrid systems (Gai et al., 2020; Pi et al., 2023), and little information is available about the effects of photosensitizers on the intracellular transfer of photoelectrons during DNRA process. Herein, metal-free CDs-500 with low cost, excellent biocompatibility and redox properties were synthesized and used to construct biohybrids for the conversion of NO3 – to NH3 under 3.7 mW cm−2 power-visible light irradiation. This study showed that, in the presence of a high NO3 – concentration (5 mM), high NO3 – conversion (>99 %) and NH3 selectivity (94.0 %) could be achieved in the strain MR-1/CDs-500 hybrid system. The generated ammonium can be further recovered by several methods such as, struvite precipitation, adsorption, membrane separation, electric field separation, ammonium stripping (Ye et al., 2018).

In summary, sludge-based redox CDs-500 were first synthesized via the 500 ℃ pyrolysis-Fenton reaction and used to construct a strain MR-1/CDs-500 hybrid system for visible-light-driven NH3 production. The addition of CDs-500 provided a short-range atypical photoelectron transfer pathway for NH3 process. During this process, the intracellular CDs-500-mediated photoelectrons were directly transferred to DNRA-related enzymes without the involvement of CymA and MtrA. Furthermore, partial photoelectrons were transferred to biomass synthesis-related enzymes by the typical reversing Mtr system. The increased biomass and upregulated related genes also indicate that the strain MR-1/CDs-500 hybrid system may be of potential platform for other biomass manufacturing.
